title: US Treasury Maintains Maximum Pressure on Iranian Oil Export Networks Through Ongoing Blockade
summary: The US Treasury Department continues enforcing sanctions against Iranian oil export networks as the naval blockade of Iranian ports persists despite the ceasefire, with OFAC tracking vessels globally to prevent sanctions evasion.
contents:
## Treasury Sanctions Enforcement Against Iranian Oil Networks

### Ongoing Enforcement
The US Department of the Treasury, through the Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC), continues to enforce maximum pressure sanctions against Iranian oil export networks. The naval blockade of the Strait of Hormuz and pursuit of Iranian-linked vessels worldwide represent the most aggressive sanctions enforcement campaign in decades.

### Economic Impact
- Russian crude and oil product exports rose by 320,000 barrels per day in March to 7.1 million bpd
- Rising global oil prices meant Russian oil export revenues nearly doubled, from $9.7 billion to $19 billion
- Iran's closure and subsequent reopening of the Strait of Hormuz triggered global energy price spikes
- Consumer price increases across a wide range of goods traced to energy market disruption

### Sanctions Architecture
The continuing blockade creates a legal gray zone between wartime operations and peacetime sanctions enforcement. Congressional Democrats argue the blockade constitutes ongoing hostilities under the War Powers Resolution, while the administration characterizes operations as sanctions enforcement.

### Global Pursuit
US forces pursue Iranian-linked oil shipments across multiple oceans, not limiting enforcement to the Persian Gulf region. This global scope reflects the extensive network of front companies, ship-to-ship transfers, and flag-of-convenience vessels employed to circumvent sanctions.

### Market Implications
The dual impact of Iran conflict disruptions and aggressive enforcement has reshaped global energy trade patterns, with oil prices hitting wartime highs. Some analysts note that US action against Iran has paradoxically benefited Russia's oil sector by driving up prices.
